title: Boundary traversal
description: Print the boundary traversal of a binary tree (left boundary, leaves, right boundary)

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (left: 4, right: 5), right: 3 (left: 6, right: 7)
examples.explanation: Step 1: Root 1 added (not leaf). Step 2: Left boundary: start at node 2 (left child of 1). Node 2 added (not leaf). Traverse left to node 4 (leaf, stop left boundary). Step 3: Leaves: DFS collects 4, 5, 6, 7 in order. Step 4: Right boundary: start at node 3 (right child of 1). Node 3 added (not leaf). Traverse right to node 7 (leaf, stop right boundary). Right boundary list [3] reversed (same). Result: [1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 3].

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (left: 4, right: null), right: 3 (left: null, right: 5)
examples.explanation: Step 1: Root 1 added. Step 2: Left boundary: node 2 added, traverse left to node 4 (leaf, stop). Step 3: Leaves: collect 4 and 5. Step 4: Right boundary: node 3 added, traverse right to node 5 (leaf, stop). Reverse [3] gives [3]. Result: [1, 2, 4, 5, 3].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive DFS with Boundary Conditions
approaches.summary: Traverse the tree recursively using DFS, applying separate conditions to collect left boundary nodes (excluding leaves), leaf nodes, and right boundary nodes (excluding leaves) in order. This approach is straightforward but requires careful handling of overlaps to avoid duplicates.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height

ladders.title: Full Problem — Boundary Traversal of Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursive DFS with boundary conditions to collect left boundary, leaves, and right boundary in order.
ladders.explanation: The boundary traversal problem requires printing nodes in a specific order: left boundary (excluding leaves), leaf nodes (left to right), and right boundary (excluding leaves, in reverse order). The recursive DFS approach elegantly handles this by separating the traversal into three distinct phases with careful condition checks to avoid duplicates. Intuition: The boundary can be visualized as walking around the tree's perimeter. We first traverse down the left side (collecting non-leaf nodes), then gather all leaves via DFS, then traverse up the right side (collecting non-leaf nodes in reverse). The key insight is that leaves belong exclusively to the leaf collection phase, not to left/right boundary phases. Step-by-step: 1. Handle empty tree case. 2. Initialize result list with root node (if not a leaf). 3. Recursively collect left boundary nodes by traversing left child preferentially, then right child if no left exists, stopping at leaf nodes. 4. Collect leaf nodes via DFS, adding only nodes with no children. 5. Collect right boundary nodes similarly to left but traversing right child preferentially, then left, and reverse the collected nodes before adding to result. Time: O(n) as each node is visited once. Space: O(h) for recursion stack, worst-case O(n) for skewed trees. Connection to L1: This integrates the three helper functions from L1 into a cohesive solution. Common mistakes: Adding leaves in left/right boundary phases (causing duplicates), forgetting to reverse right boundary, mishandling single-node trees. Real code pattern: def boundary_traversal(root): if not root: return []; result = [root.val] if not is_leaf(root) else []; add_left_boundary(root.left, result); add_leaves(root, result); add_right_boundary(root.right, result); return result.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (left: 4, right: 5), right: 3 (left: 6, right: 7)
examples.trace: Step 1: Root 1 added (not leaf). Step 2: Left boundary: start at node 2 (left child of 1). Node 2 added (not leaf). Traverse left to node 4 (leaf, stop left boundary). Step 3: Leaves: DFS collects 4, 5, 6, 7 in order. Step 4: Right boundary: start at node 3 (right child of 1). Node 3 added (not leaf). Traverse right to node 7 (leaf, stop right boundary). Right boundary list [3] reversed (same). Result: [1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 3].

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (left: 4, right: null), right: 3 (left: null, right: 5)
examples.trace: Step 1: Root 1 added. Step 2: Left boundary: node 2 added, traverse left to node 4 (leaf, stop). Step 3: Leaves: collect 4 and 5. Step 4: Right boundary: node 3 added, traverse right to node 5 (leaf, stop). Reverse [3] gives [3]. Result: [1, 2, 4, 5, 3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Three Helper Functions for Boundary Collection
ladders.desc: Implement separate recursive functions to collect left boundary, leaves, and right boundary nodes.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine breaks the full problem into three manageable parts: left boundary collection, leaf collection, and right boundary collection. Intuition: Each boundary type has distinct traversal rules, so separating them simplifies logic and avoids duplication. Step-by-step: 1. add_left_boundary(node, result): If node is null or leaf, return. Add node value to result. Recursively call with left child if exists, else right child. 2. add_leaves(node, result): If node is null, return. If node is leaf, add value. Recursively call left then right children. 3. add_right_boundary(node, result, temp): Similar to left but uses a temporary list to collect nodes, then reverses it before adding to result. Time: O(n) total for all helpers. Space: O(h) recursion. Connection to L0: These helpers are called in sequence in L0. Common mistakes: Not checking leaf condition in left/right helpers, forgetting to reverse right boundary. Real code pattern: def add_left_boundary(node, res): if not node or is_leaf(node): return; res.append(node.val); if node.left: add_left_boundary(node.left, res); else: add_left_boundary(node.right, res).

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (left: 4), right: 3. Call add_left_boundary on node 2.
examples.trace: Node 2 is not leaf, add 2. Left child 4 exists, call add_left_boundary(4). Node 4 is leaf, return. Result: [2].

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (right: 5), right: 3. Call add_leaves on root 1.
examples.trace: Start at 1 (not leaf). Recurse left to 2 (not leaf). Recurse left to null, return. Recurse right to 5 (leaf), add 5. Back to 2, done. Back to 1, recurse right to 3 (leaf), add 3. Result: [5, 3].

testCases.input: add_left_boundary on node 2 with left child 4
add_left_boundary on node 2 with right child 5 only
add_leaves on node 1 with children 2 and 3 (both leaves)
add_right_boundary on node 3 with right child 7
add_right_boundary on node 3 with left child 6 only

ladders.title: Core Logic — DFS Traversal with Boundary Conditions
ladders.desc: Use DFS to traverse tree, applying conditions to identify and collect boundary nodes without duplicates.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves traversing the tree using DFS while applying specific conditions to determine if a node belongs to left boundary, leaves, or right boundary. Intuition: DFS naturally visits nodes in a depth-first order, but we need to modify it to collect nodes based on their position relative to the boundary. Key conditions: For left boundary, collect nodes that are on the leftmost path (prefer left child, fallback to right) but stop at leaves. For leaves, collect any node with no children. For right boundary, similar to left but prefer right child and reverse order. Step-by-step: Traverse recursively, at each node check: if it's a left boundary candidate (based on parent direction), add it; if leaf, add to leaf list; if right boundary candidate, add to temp list. Time: O(n). Space: O(h). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the three helper functions. Common mistakes: Adding nodes multiple times by not excluding leaves from left/right boundaries. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, is_left_boundary, is_right_boundary): if not node: return; if is_left_boundary or is_leaf(node) or is_right_boundary: add to appropriate list; recurse with updated flags.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3. DFS with boundary flags.
examples.trace: Start at 1: is_left_boundary=True, add to left. Recurse left to 2: is_left_boundary=True, add to left. 2 has no children (leaf), but since is_left_boundary, not added to leaves. Recurse right to 3: is_right_boundary=True, add to right. Result lists as above.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (left: 4), right: 3. DFS with flags.
examples.trace: At 1: add to left. At 2: add to left. At 4: is leaf, add to leaves. At 3: add to right.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal and Leaf Detection
ladders.desc: Perform DFS traversal on a binary tree and identify leaf nodes.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves two key skills: traversing a binary tree using DFS (Depth-First Search) and detecting leaf nodes. Intuition: DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, which is ideal for visiting all nodes. A leaf node is defined as having no left and right children. Step-by-step: For DFS, use recursion: visit node, recursively traverse left subtree, then right subtree. For leaf detection, check if both left and right children are null. Time: O(n) for traversal. Space: O(h) for recursion. Connection to L2: This is the foundation for applying boundary conditions during traversal. Common mistakes: Forgetting base case in DFS (null node), incorrect leaf check (e.g., checking only one child). Real code pattern: def dfs(node): if not node: return; process node; dfs(node.left); dfs(node.right). def is_leaf(node): return node and not node.left and not node.right.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3. DFS traversal.
examples.trace: Start at 1, visit 1. Recurse left to 2, visit 2. 2 has no children, return. Recurse right to 3, visit 3. Done.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (left: 4), right: 3. Check leaves.
examples.trace: Node 1: left and right exist, not leaf. Node 2: left exists, not leaf. Node 4: no children, leaf. Node 3: no children, leaf.

testCases.input: DFS on tree with single node 1
is_leaf on node with value 1 and no children
is_leaf on node with left child only
is_leaf on node with right child only
is_leaf on node with both children
DFS and leaf check on tree: 1 (left: 2 (right: 5), right: 3)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Function Calls on Tree Nodes
ladders.desc: Implement recursive functions to process binary tree nodes, including base cases and child calls.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of writing recursive functions for tree structures. Intuition: Recursion is natural for trees due to their hierarchical nature—each node can be seen as the root of a subtree. Step-by-step: Define a function that takes a tree node as input. Base case: if node is null, return (stop recursion). Recursive case: process the current node (e.g., print value), then call the function recursively on left child and right child. Time and space depend on implementation but typically O(n) and O(h). Connection to L3: This operation is used in DFS traversal. Common mistakes: Missing base case leading to infinite recursion, not handling null children properly. Real code pattern: def process_tree(node): if node is None: return; print(node.val); process_tree(node.left); process_tree(node.right).

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3. Recursive function to print values.
examples.trace: Call process_tree(1): node not null, print 1. Call process_tree(2): print 2. 2 has no children, calls return. Call process_tree(3): print 3. Done.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (left: 4), right: null. Recursive print.
examples.trace: Call process_tree(1): print 1. Call process_tree(2): print 2. Call process_tree(4): print 4. 4 has no children, return. Back to 2, right child null, return. Back to 1, right child null, return.

testCases.input: Tree: 1 (left: 2 (left: 4, right: 5), right: 3 (left: 6, right: 7)), recursive print

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Null Checks
ladders.desc: Understand binary tree node representation and perform basic null checks.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the basic structure of a binary tree node and the importance of null checks. Intuition: A binary tree is composed of nodes, where each node has a value and references to left and right children. Null represents the absence of a child or an empty tree. Step-by-step: Define a node class with attributes: val, left, right. In operations, always check if a node is null before accessing its attributes to avoid errors. Time and space are O(1) for these checks. Connection to L4: Null checks are the base case in recursive functions. Common mistakes: Accessing left or right on a null node, not initializing children as null. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. To check: if node is None: handle empty case.

examples.input: Create a node with value 1 and no children.
examples.trace: Instantiate TreeNode(1). Set left and right to None by default.

examples.input: Check if a node is null before printing its value.
examples.output: If node is null, print 'Empty'; else print value.
examples.trace: Given node = None, check: node is None, so print 'Empty'. Given node with val=2, check: node is not None, print 2.

testCases.input: Node with val=10, left=node with val=3, right=null
Null check on node with val=7
Access left child of null node
Access right child of node with val=2 and right=null

testCases.expected: root val=1, left val=2, right val=3

testCases.input: Check if node with val=4 and both children null is leaf
Node with val=6, left=null, right=node with val=9
Handle null in recursive base case
testCases.expected: Return early if node is null

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative Level-Order with Node Tracking
approaches.summary: Perform a level-order traversal using a queue to identify boundary nodes by tracking the first and last nodes at each level, combined with a separate pass to collect leaves. This method leverages breadth-first search to systematically capture boundaries.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Boundary Traversal Using Iterative Level-Order with Node Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print boundary traversal of a binary tree using level-order traversal with first/last node tracking and leaf collection.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the boundary traversal problem by combining three systematic passes: left boundary, leaves, and right boundary. The intuition is that boundary nodes can be identified through a breadth-first level-order traversal where we track the first and last nodes at each level. The left boundary consists of the first node at each level (excluding leaves already captured), the right boundary consists of the last node at each level (in reverse order, excluding leaves and left boundary duplicates), and leaves are collected separately through a simple traversal. The algorithm works because level-order traversal naturally exposes the outermost nodes at each depth, which correspond to boundary positions when combined with proper filtering. Step-by-step: 1. Handle empty tree case. 2. Initialize result list with root (if not leaf). 3. Perform level-order traversal using a queue. For each level, track first and last nodes. Add first nodes to left boundary (excluding leaves). Store last nodes for later. 4. Collect all leaf nodes through any traversal (e.g., inorder). 5. Add leaf nodes to result. 6. Add right boundary nodes in reverse order from stored last nodes (excluding leaves and left boundary duplicates). 7. Return result. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse each node a constant number of times. Space complexity is O(n) for queue storage and result lists. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete, efficient solution. Common mistakes include: duplicate nodes (especially leaves appearing in multiple sections), incorrect ordering of right boundary (must be reverse), and mishandling single-node trees. Real code pattern: def boundary_traversal(root): if not root: return []; result = [root.val] if not (not root.left and not root.right) else []; queue = deque([root]); last_nodes = []; while queue: level_size = len(queue); for i in range(level_size): node = queue.popleft(); if node.left: queue.append(node.left); if node.right: queue.append(node.right); if i == 0 and not (not node.left and not node.right): result.append(node.val); if i == level_size-1: last_nodes.append(node); leaves = []; def collect_leaves(node): if not node: return; if not node.left and not node.right: leaves.append(node.val); collect_leaves(node.left); collect_leaves(node.right); collect_leaves(root); result.extend(leaves); for node in reversed(last_nodes[1:-1] if len(last_nodes)>2 else last_nodes[1:]): if not (not node.left and not node.right) and node.val not in result: result.append(node.val); return result.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2 (left child of 1), right: 3 (right child of 1), 2 has left: 4, right: 5, 3 has left: 6, right: 7
examples.trace: Step 1: Root 1 added to result (not leaf). Step 2: Level-order traversal: Level 0: first node 1 (already added), last node 1 (store). Level 1: nodes [2,3], first node 2 (add to left boundary), last node 3 (store). Level 2: nodes [4,5,6,7], first node 4 (add to left boundary), last node 7 (store). Step 3: Collect leaves: inorder traversal finds 4,5,6,7 as leaves. Step 4: Add leaves to result: result becomes [1,2,4,4,5,6,7]. Step 5: Add right boundary in reverse from stored last nodes [1,3,7]: skip root 1 (already added), add 3 (not leaf, not duplicate), skip 7 (leaf already added). Final result: [1,2,4,5,6,7,3].

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: null, 2 has left: 4, right: 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Root 1 added (not leaf). Step 2: Level-order: Level 0: first 1 (added), last 1 (store). Level 1: nodes [2], first 2 (add to left boundary), last 2 (store). Level 2: nodes [4,5], first 4 (add to left boundary), last 5 (store). Step 3: Collect leaves: 4 and 5 are leaves. Step 4: Add leaves: result [1,2,4,4,5]. Step 5: Right boundary from stored [1,2,5]: skip 1, skip 2 (already in left boundary), skip 5 (leaf already added). Final: [1,2,4,5].

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: null, 2 left: 3, 2 right: 4, 3 left: 5
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2 left: 4, 2 right: 5, 4 left: 6, 5 right: 7
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2 left: 4, 3 left: 5, 3 right: 6, 5 left: 7

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Level-Order Traversal with First/Last Node Tracking
ladders.desc: Perform level-order traversal using a queue while recording the first and last node at each level.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the boundary traversal approach, as it identifies potential boundary nodes through breadth-first search. The intuition is that in a level-order traversal, the first node at each level corresponds to the leftmost visible node from that depth, which may be part of the left boundary, while the last node corresponds to the rightmost visible node, potentially part of the right boundary. By tracking these during traversal, we efficiently capture boundary candidates without additional passes. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize queue with root. 2. While queue not empty: a. Get level size. b. For i from 0 to level_size-1: dequeue node, process children. c. If i == 0, record as first node. d. If i == level_size-1, record as last node. Time complexity O(n), space O(n) for queue. This feeds into L0 by providing the left boundary nodes and stored last nodes for right boundary. Common mistakes: not resetting level size each iteration, missing single-level trees. Real code pattern: def track_level_nodes(root): if not root: return [], []; queue = deque([root]); first_nodes = []; last_nodes = []; while queue: level_size = len(queue); for i in range(level_size): node = queue.popleft(); if node.left: queue.append(node.left); if node.right: queue.append(node.right); if i == 0: first_nodes.append(node); if i == level_size-1: last_nodes.append(node); return first_nodes, last_nodes.

examples.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue [1]. Level 0: size 1, i=0, node 1, first_nodes add 1, last_nodes add 1. Step 2: Queue [2,3]. Level 1: size 2, i=0 node 2 (first_nodes add 2), i=1 node 3 (last_nodes add 3). Final: first_nodes [1,2], last_nodes [1,3].

examples.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: null, 2 left: 3, 2 right: 4
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue [1]. Level 0: size 1, node 1, first add 1, last add 1. Step 2: Queue [2]. Level 1: size 1, node 2, first add 2, last add 2. Step 3: Queue [3,4]. Level 2: size 2, i=0 node 3 (first add 3), i=1 node 4 (last add 4). Final: first_nodes [1,2,3], last_nodes [1,2,4].

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2 left: 4, 3 right: 5, 4 left: 6
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2 left: 4, 2 right: 5, 4 left: 6, 5 right: 7

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identifying Boundary Nodes from Level-Order Results
ladders.desc: Filter first and last nodes from level-order traversal to extract left and right boundary nodes, excluding leaves.
ladders.explanation: This core logic transforms the tracked first and last nodes into actual boundary nodes by applying filtering rules. The intuition is that not all first/last nodes are boundary nodes: leaves should be excluded (as they are collected separately), and duplicates must be avoided. For left boundary: take first nodes from each level, but skip any that are leaves. For right boundary: take last nodes from each level in reverse order, skipping leaves and nodes already in left boundary. This works because the boundary definition requires non-leaf nodes for left/right boundaries, with leaves handled separately. Step-by-step: 1. Input: first_nodes list, last_nodes list from level-order. 2. Left boundary: iterate through first_nodes, add node.val if node is not a leaf. 3. Right boundary: iterate through last_nodes in reverse, starting from second-last to avoid root duplicate, add node.val if node is not a leaf and not already in left boundary. Time O(n), space O(n). This connects to L1 by processing its output. Common mistakes: including leaves, incorrect reverse order, missing root handling. Real code pattern: def extract_boundaries(first_nodes, last_nodes): left = []; for node in first_nodes: if not (not node.left and not node.right): left.append(node.val); right = []; for node in reversed(last_nodes[1:]): if not (not node.left and not node.right) and node.val not in left: right.append(node.val); return left, right.

examples.input: First nodes: [1,2,4], Last nodes: [1,3,5], where 4 and 5 are leaves
examples.trace: Step 1: Left boundary: node 1 (not leaf) add 1, node 2 (not leaf) add 2, node 4 (leaf) skip. Left = [1,2]. Step 2: Right boundary: reverse last_nodes [5,3,1], skip root 1 (index handling), node 5 (leaf) skip, node 3 (not leaf, not in left) add 3. Right = [3].

examples.input: First nodes: [1,2,3], Last nodes: [1,2,3], all nodes non-leaf except maybe leaves
examples.trace: Step 1: Left: node 1 add 1, node 2 add 2, node 3 add 3 (assuming non-leaf). Left = [1,2,3]. Step 2: Right: reverse last_nodes [3,2,1], skip root, node 3 in left skip, node 2 in left skip. Right = [].

testCases.input: First: [1,2,3], Last: [1,2,4], nodes all not leaf

ladders.title: Building Block — Collecting Leaf Nodes in a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Traverse a binary tree to collect all leaf node values in left-to-right order.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for the boundary traversal, as leaves form the middle section of the output. The intuition is that leaves can be collected through any tree traversal (e.g., inorder, preorder, postorder) by checking if a node has no children. Using an inorder traversal ensures leaves are collected in left-to-right order, matching the problem requirement. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty list for leaves. 2. Define recursive function: if node is null, return; if node has no left and no right child, add node.val to list; recursively call on left child, then right child. 3. Call function on root. Time O(n), space O(h) for recursion stack. This connects to L2 by providing the leaf list to integrate with boundaries. Common mistakes: wrong order (use inorder for left-to-right), missing null checks. Real code pattern: def collect_leaves(root): leaves = []; def inorder(node): if not node: return; inorder(node.left); if not node.left and not node.right: leaves.append(node.val); inorder(node.right); inorder(root); return leaves.

examples.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Inorder traversal: go to left child 2, it has no children so add 2, back to root 1, go to right child 3, add 3. Leaves = [2,3].

examples.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: null, 2 left: 3, 2 right: 4
examples.trace: Step 1: Inorder: go to 2, then to left 3 (add 3), back to 2, then to right 4 (add 4). Leaves = [3,4].

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2 left: 4, 3 right: 5, 4 left: 6
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2 left: 4, 2 right: 5, 4 left: 6, 5 right: 7

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Level-Order Traversal of a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Traverse a binary tree level by level using a queue.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to the approach, as it enables systematic access to nodes by depth. The intuition is that a queue (FIFO structure) allows processing nodes in the order they are encountered, naturally producing level-order output. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize queue with root. 2. While queue not empty: a. Dequeue node. b. Process node (e.g., print value). c. Enqueue left child if exists. d. Enqueue right child if exists. Time O(n), space O(n). This connects to L3 by providing the traversal framework for tracking nodes. Common mistakes: not checking for null children, infinite loops. Real code pattern: def level_order(root): if not root: return; queue = deque([root]); while queue: node = queue.popleft(); print(node.val); if node.left: queue.append(node.left); if node.right: queue.append(node.right).

examples.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue [1], dequeue 1 print 1, enqueue 2 and 3. Step 2: Queue [2,3], dequeue 2 print 2, enqueue nothing, dequeue 3 print 3. Output: 1 2 3.

examples.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: null, 2 left: 3, 2 right: 4
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue [1], dequeue 1 print 1, enqueue 2. Step 2: Queue [2], dequeue 2 print 2, enqueue 3 and 4. Step 3: Queue [3,4], dequeue 3 print 3, dequeue 4 print 4. Output: 1 2 3 4.

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2 left: 4, 3 right: 5, 4 left: 6
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2 left: 4, 2 right: 5, 4 left: 6, 5 right: 7

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Queue Usage
ladders.desc: Understand binary tree node representation and basic queue operations for traversal.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the basic building blocks: binary trees and queues. A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right). Nodes are typically represented with attributes for value, left child, and right child. Queues are FIFO (First-In-First-Out) data structures used for level-order traversal because they process nodes in the order they are added, mimicking breadth-first exploration. Intuition: by starting at the root and adding children to a queue, we ensure nodes at the same depth are processed together. Step-by-step: 1. Define a TreeNode class with val, left, right. 2. Initialize a queue (e.g., using list or deque). 3. Enqueue root. 4. While queue not empty: dequeue node, access its value and children. Time O(1) per operation, space O(n). This connects to L4 by providing the structural knowledge needed for traversal. Common mistakes: not initializing queue properly, confusing stacks and queues. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. Queue usage: from collections import deque; q = deque(); q.append(root); while q: node = q.popleft().

examples.input: Create tree: root val 1, left child val 2, right child val 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Define nodes: node1 = TreeNode(1); node2 = TreeNode(2); node3 = TreeNode(3). Step 2: Set links: node1.left = node2; node1.right = node3. Step 3: Queue init: q = deque([node1]). Step 4: Dequeue node1, access val 1, left child node2, right child node3.

examples.input: Queue with nodes 1,2,3
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue = deque([1,2,3]). Step 2: popleft() returns 1, queue now [2,3]. Step 3: popleft() returns 2, queue [3]. Step 4: popleft() returns 3, queue empty.

testCases.expected: Node1 val 1 left=Node2 val 2 right=Node3 val 3

testCases.input: Queue with nodes a,b,c, dequeue all

approaches.name: Approach 3: Three-Phase Iterative Traversal
approaches.summary: Break the problem into three separate phases: traverse left boundary (top-down), collect all leaves (using any traversal), and traverse right boundary (bottom-up). This modular approach simplifies implementation by handling each part independently.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) for recursion or stack in leaf collection

ladders.title: Full Problem — Boundary Traversal Using Three-Phase Iterative Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print boundary traversal of a binary tree using three separate phases.
ladders.explanation: The boundary traversal problem requires printing nodes in a specific order: left boundary (excluding leaves, top-down), all leaf nodes (left to right), and right boundary (excluding leaves, bottom-up). The three-phase approach breaks this complex problem into manageable parts. Intuition: Instead of trying to handle all boundary cases in one complex traversal, we separate concerns. The left boundary can be collected by following left children until reaching a leaf, with right children as fallbacks when left is absent. Leaves can be collected using any tree traversal that visits nodes in order. The right boundary requires a bottom-up traversal, which is naturally achieved by recursion or by reversing a top-down collection. This modular design makes each phase simpler to implement and debug. Step-by-step: 1. Handle empty tree case. 2. Initialize result list with root node (if not a leaf). 3. Phase 1: Traverse left boundary from root.left to leaf, adding non-leaf nodes. Use a while loop: at each node, if it has a left child, go left; else if it has a right child, go right; else stop (reached leaf). 4. Phase 2: Collect all leaf nodes using a helper function (e.g., DFS). Traverse tree, and for each node, if it has no left and no right child, add to result. 5. Phase 3: Traverse right boundary from root.right to leaf, collecting non-leaf nodes in reverse order. Use similar logic as left boundary but collect nodes in a temporary list, then reverse and append to result. Time complexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes, as each node is visited at most twice (once in leaf collection, possibly in boundary traversals). Space complexity: O(h) for recursion stack in leaf collection, where h is tree height. Connection to L1: This solution directly uses the leaf collection helper from L1 as Phase 2. Common mistakes: Including leaves in left/right boundaries (should exclude), missing root node in result, incorrect order for right boundary (must be bottom-up), and not handling single-node trees properly. Real code pattern: def boundary_traversal(root): if not root: return []; result = [root.val] if not (not root.left and not root.right) else []; # Left boundary; node = root.left; while node: if node.left or node.right: result.append(node.val); if node.left: node = node.left; else: node = node.right; else: break; # Leaves; def add_leaves(node): if not node: return; if not node.left and not node.right: result.append(node.val); else: add_leaves(node.left); add_leaves(node.right); add_leaves(root); # Right boundary; right_nodes = []; node = root.right; while node: if node.left or node.right: right_nodes.append(node.val); if node.right: node = node.right; else: node = node.left; else: break; result.extend(reversed(right_nodes)); return result.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7; all leaves: 4,5,6,7
examples.trace: Step 1: Root 1 is not leaf, add to result: [1]. Step 2: Left boundary from root.left=2: node=2, has children, add 2: [1,2]; node has left child 4, go left; node=4, is leaf (no children), stop left boundary. Step 3: Collect leaves via DFS: start at root 1, not leaf; go left to 2, not leaf; go left to 4, leaf, add 4: [1,2,4]; backtrack to 2, go right to 5, leaf, add 5: [1,2,4,5]; backtrack to 1, go right to 3, not leaf; go left to 6, leaf, add 6: [1,2,4,5,6]; go right to 7, leaf, add 7: [1,2,4,5,6,7]. Step 4: Right boundary from root.right=3: node=3, has children, add to temp list: [3]; node has right child 7, go right; node=7, is leaf, stop; reverse temp list [3] and append: result becomes [1,2,4,5,6,7,3]. Final: [1,2,4,5,6,7,3].

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: null; 2 has left: 3, right: 4; 3 and 4 are leaves
examples.trace: Step 1: Root 1 not leaf, add: [1]. Step 2: Left boundary from root.left=2: node=2, has children, add 2: [1,2]; node has left child 3, go left; node=3, leaf, stop. Step 3: Collect leaves: DFS from root 1, not leaf; go left to 2, not leaf; go left to 3, leaf, add 3: [1,2,3]; backtrack to 2, go right to 4, leaf, add 4: [1,2,3,4]. Step 4: Right boundary from root.right=null, skip. Final: [1,2,3,4].

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: null; 3 has left: null, right: 5; leaves: 4,5
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7; 4 has left: 8, right: 9; leaves: 8,9,5,6,7
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: null; 2 has left: null, right: 3; 3 is leaf
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: null; 4 is leaf; 3 has left: null, right: 5; 5 is leaf
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 4 has left: 6, right: null; 6 is leaf; 5 is leaf; 3 has left: 7, right: 8; 7 is leaf; 8 has left: null, right: 9; 9 is leaf
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7; 4 has left: 8, right: 9; 8 and 9 are leaves; 5 is leaf; 6 is leaf; 7 has left: 10, right: null; 10 is leaf
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: null, right: 4; 3 has left: 5, right: null; 4 is leaf; 5 is leaf

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Collecting All Leaf Nodes in a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Implement a function to collect all leaf nodes of a binary tree in left-to-right order.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is Phase 2 of the boundary traversal, responsible for gathering all leaf nodes. Intuition: Leaf nodes are those with no children (left and right are null). To collect them in left-to-right order, we need a traversal that visits nodes in an order that respects the tree's structure—typically an inorder, preorder, or postorder traversal works, as long as we process left subtree before right subtree. This ensures leaves from left parts appear before those from right parts. Step-by-step: Use a recursive DFS approach. Base case: if node is null, return. Recursive case: if node is a leaf (both children null), add its value to a result list. Otherwise, recursively call on left child, then on right child. This yields leaves in left-to-right order because we explore left subtree fully before right subtree. Time complexity: O(n) where n is number of nodes, as we visit each node once. Space complexity: O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height. Connection to L0: This function is directly called in the full solution to handle the leaf collection phase. Common mistakes: Using BFS which doesn't guarantee left-to-right order for leaves across subtrees, forgetting to handle null nodes, or including non-leaf nodes. Real code pattern: def collect_leaves(root, result): if not root: return; if not root.left and not root.right: result.append(root.val); else: collect_leaves(root.left, result); collect_leaves(root.right, result).

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7; all leaves: 4,5,6,7
examples.trace: Start at root 1: not leaf, go left to 2; 2 not leaf, go left to 4; 4 is leaf, add 4; backtrack to 2, go right to 5; 5 is leaf, add 5; backtrack to 1, go right to 3; 3 not leaf, go left to 6; 6 is leaf, add 6; go right to 7; 7 is leaf, add 7. Result: [4,5,6,7].

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root only, leaf)
examples.trace: Root 1 has no children, so it is a leaf. Add 1. Result: [1].

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: null; 3 has left: null, right: 5; leaves: 4,5
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7; 4 has left: 8, right: 9; leaves: 8,9,5,6,7

ladders.title: Core Logic — Traversing Left and Right Boundaries of a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Learn to traverse the left boundary (top-down) and right boundary (bottom-up) of a binary tree, excluding leaves.
ladders.explanation: This core logic handles Phases 1 and 3 of the boundary traversal. Intuition: The left boundary consists of nodes on the path from the root's left child to the leftmost leaf, but excluding the leaf itself. Similarly, the right boundary is from the root's right child to the rightmost leaf, excluding the leaf, and must be printed in reverse order. For left boundary: start at root.left, and at each node, if it has a left child, go left; else if it has a right child, go right; else stop (reached a leaf). Add non-leaf nodes to result as we go. This ensures we follow the outermost left edge. For right boundary: same logic but start at root.right and prefer right child over left, and collect nodes in a temporary list to reverse later for bottom-up order. Why exclude leaves? Leaves are handled separately in Phase 2 to avoid duplication. Step-by-step for left boundary: initialize node = root.left; while node is not null and (node.left or node.right): add node.val to result; if node.left exists: node = node.left; else: node = node.right. For right boundary: node = root.right; temp_list = []; while node is not null and (node.left or node.right): add node.val to temp_list; if node.right exists: node = node.right; else: node = node.left; then reverse temp_list and append to result. Time complexity: O(h) where h is tree height, as we traverse at most height nodes. Space complexity: O(1) for left boundary, O(h) for right boundary if using list. Connection to L0: These traversals form two of the three phases in the full solution. Common mistakes: Including leaves, not handling cases where a node has only one child correctly, or forgetting to reverse the right boundary. Real code pattern: For left: while node and (node.left or node.right): result.append(node.val); node = node.left if node.left else node.right. For right: while node and (node.left or node.right): temp.append(node.val); node = node.right if node.right else node.left; result.extend(reversed(temp)).

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7; leaves: 4,5,6,7
examples.output: Left boundary: [2]; Right boundary: [3] (after reverse, but as collected: [3])
examples.trace: Left boundary: start at root.left=2; node=2 has children, add 2; node has left child 4, go left; node=4 is leaf (no children), stop. Result: [2]. Right boundary: start at root.right=3; node=3 has children, add to temp: [3]; node has right child 7, go right; node=7 is leaf, stop; reverse temp (already [3]), append: [3].

examples.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: null; 2 has left: 3, right: 4; 3 and 4 are leaves
examples.trace: Left boundary: start at root.left=2; node=2 has children, add 2; node has left child 3, go left; node=3 is leaf, stop. Result: [2]. Right boundary: root.right is null, skip.

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; all leaves, left boundary
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: null; 3 has left: null, right: 5; leaves: 4,5, left boundary
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 3 has left: 6, right: 7; leaves: 4,5,6,7, right boundary (as collected before reverse)
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: null; 2 has left: null, right: 3; 3 is leaf, left boundary
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: null; 4 is leaf; 3 has left: null, right: 5; 5 is leaf, right boundary (as collected)
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; 4 has left: 6, right: null; 6 is leaf; 5 is leaf; 3 has left: 7, right: 8; 7 is leaf; 8 has left: null, right: 9; 9 is leaf, left boundary

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal Algorithms (DFS and BFS)
ladders.desc: Understand Depth-First Search (DFS) and Breadth-First Search (BFS) for binary trees.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for Phase 2 (leaf collection) and general tree manipulation. Intuition: Tree traversal algorithms systematically visit all nodes in a tree. DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, using recursion or a stack. BFS explores level by level, using a queue. For boundary traversal, DFS is preferred for leaf collection because it naturally respects the left-to-right order when implemented recursively (visit left subtree before right). DFS variants: Preorder (root, left, right), Inorder (left, root, right), Postorder (left, right, root)—all work for leaf collection if we add leaves when encountered. BFS could also collect leaves but might not maintain left-to-right order across different depths if not careful. Step-by-step DFS recursive: def dfs(node): if not node: return; # process node (e.g., check if leaf); dfs(node.left); dfs(node.right). Step-by-step BFS iterative: queue = [root]; while queue: node = queue.pop(0); # process node; if node.left: queue.append(node.left); if node.right: queue.append(node.right). Time complexity: O(n) for both, visiting each node once. Space complexity: O(h) for DFS recursion stack, O(w) for BFS queue where w is maximum width. Connection to L2: DFS is used in leaf collection, and understanding traversal helps implement boundary walks. Common mistakes: Infinite recursion if base case missing, not handling null nodes, or using BFS when order matters. Real code pattern: For DFS leaf collection: def dfs(node, result): if not node: return; if not node.left and not node.right: result.append(node.val); dfs(node.left, result); dfs(node.right, result).

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3; DFS preorder traversal
examples.trace: Start at root 1: process 1; go left to 2: process 2; 2 has no children, backtrack; go right to 3: process 3; 3 has no children, done. Order: 1,2,3.

examples.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 has left: 4, right: 5; BFS traversal
examples.trace: Queue start: [1]; dequeue 1, process 1, enqueue children 2 and 3: queue [2,3]; dequeue 2, process 2, enqueue children 4 and 5: queue [3,4,5]; dequeue 3, process 3, no children: queue [4,5]; dequeue 4, process 4, no children: queue [5]; dequeue 5, process 5, done. Order: 1,2,3,4,5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Identifying Leaf Nodes in a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Determine if a given node in a binary tree is a leaf node.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental for Phase 2 (leaf collection) and for excluding leaves in boundary traversals. Intuition: A leaf node in a binary tree has no children—i.e., both its left and right pointers are null (or None in Python). This is a simple condition check that forms the core of many tree algorithms. Why is this important? In boundary traversal, we need to separate leaf nodes from boundary nodes to avoid duplication and ensure correct order. Step-by-step: Given a tree node, check if it is null; if so, it's not a leaf (or handle as empty). Otherwise, check if node.left is null and node.right is null. If both are null, the node is a leaf; otherwise, it is not. Time complexity: O(1) constant time for a single check. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L3: This check is used within DFS/BFS traversals to collect leaves. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check both children, or misidentifying null nodes as leaves. Real code pattern: def is_leaf(node): return node is not None and node.left is None and node.right is None.

examples.input: Node with value 1, left: null, right: null
examples.trace: Node is not null. Check left child: null. Check right child: null. Both are null, so it is a leaf.

examples.input: Node with value 2, left: node with value 3, right: null
examples.trace: Node is not null. Check left child: exists (not null). Since left is not null, node is not a leaf (even though right is null).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Navigation
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a binary tree node and how to access its children.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite for working with binary trees. Intuition: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Each node typically contains a value and references (pointers) to its left and right children. Understanding this structure is essential for any tree traversal or manipulation. In Python, a node can be represented as a class with attributes: val (the value), left (reference to left child node), and right (reference to right child node). Navigation: Given a node, you can access its left child via node.left and right child via node.right. If a child does not exist, it is null (None in Python). This allows us to move through the tree. Step-by-step: Define a TreeNode class: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. To navigate: start at root; to go left, set current = current.left; to go right, set current = current.right. Always check for null before accessing to avoid errors. Time complexity: O(1) for accessing children. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L4: This foundation enables checking if a node is a leaf by examining its children. Common mistakes: Not initializing children as None, or trying to access attributes of a null node. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: as above; node = TreeNode(1); left_child = TreeNode(2); node.left = left_child; to access: if node.left: print(node.left.val).

examples.input: Create a tree node with value 10, left child with value 5, right child with value 15
examples.trace: Step 1: Create root node: TreeNode(10). Step 2: Create left child: TreeNode(5). Step 3: Create right child: TreeNode(15). Step 4: Assign: root.left = left child; root.right = right child. Result: root has val 10, left points to node with val 5, right points to node with val 15.

examples.input: Given root node with value 1 and left child with value 2, navigate to left child and print its value
examples.trace: Start at root: node.val is 1. Check if node.left exists: yes, it is a TreeNode with val 2. Set node = node.left. Now node.val is 2. Print 2.
